title: rootless podman fails to mount 'sys/fs/cgroup' w/ system-wide umask of 0027 and userns={auto|nomap}
body:
### Issue Description

With a system-wide umask of 0027 (through login.defs/pam_umask), running a rootless container with podman and `userns` either set to `auto` or `nomap` fails with the following error:

```
$ podman run --userns=nomap --rm -it alpine /bin/sh
Error: crun: mount `cgroup2` to `sys/fs/cgroup`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

Here a few additional observations:

- With a system-wide umask of 0022 (pam_umask), but the current session/shell set to 0027, it works though.
- The same container that failed previously with 0027, works if system-wide umask is set to 0022.
- Native or fuse overlayfs both behave the same (fuse overlayfs is preferred though due to the hugely expensive upfront chown with native).
- Setting `--cgroups=disabled` starts the container even with a system-wide umask of 0027 (but that's not really a solution).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Set the system-wide umask to 0027 (with pam_umask/login.defs)
2. As a user, run ` podman run --userns=nomap --rm -it alpine /bin/sh` or `podman run --userns=auto --rm -it alpine /bin/sh`


### Describe the results you received

The container will fail to run with the following error:

```
Error: crun: mount `cgroup2` to `sys/fs/cgroup`: Permission denied: OCI permission denied
```

### Describe the results you expected

The container should run normally, irrespective of the system-wide umask setting.

### podman info output

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

I have tested with podman versions 5.4.2/5.3.2/5.3.0/git head, and all fail with the same error.

comments.body: are you able to run something like `systemd-run --scope --user echo hello` with that configuration?

Absoutely-- if that broke, I would notice rather quickly. 😄 

Quite frankly, as far as I can remember, I really never had any problems with that setup in the past... except for the (repeated) exception of podman.

I am not sure what we can do here.  It seems that the current cgroup root must be accessible before we can mount the cgroup2 file system.   We can't/shouldn't manually modify the cgroup that systemd has created and we cannot specify `User=` for the systemd scope since it runs outside of the user namespace.

An easier reproducer that does not involve Podman:

```
$ systemd-run --scope --user unshare --keep-caps -S 0 -G 0 --map-auto -Cm mount -t cgroup2 cgroup /sys/fs/cgroup
Running as unit: run-p4287-i4288.scope; invocation ID: bf7bdb560af14a22900b7d09cd1fe5d4
mount: /sys/fs/cgroup: cannot mount cgroup read-only.
       dmesg(1) may have more information after failed mount system call.
```

Which is exactly the error I am seeing in the OCI runtime

I see. That's a pity. It kinda looks to me as it is working as intended in this case... even though it is not quite what is desired. So there is probably no use in getting the systemd folks involved?

As a workaround, I can simply disable cgroups (`--cgroups=disabled`). Are there any security implications in doing so? Clearly, I can no longer limit resources, but is podman/crun using those cgroups for something "important" behind the scenes that shouldn't be disabled?

Also, I haven't tested that yet, but I should be able to set resource limits that hold through running podman via `systemd-run` which would be a workaround to get those back.

Last but not least, it would be nice to get some better diagnostics from podman/crun when mounting fails. It took me quite some time to figure out what exactly was going on and what the "culprit" was.

I don't see what systemd could do here, but I see some possible workarounds:

- use cgroupfs.
- map your user inside the user namespace (no `nomap`), even if you don't run the process with this UID/GID
- manually fix the cgroup directories permissions (`chmod 755 -R /sys/fs/cgroup/user.slice/user-1000.slice`), you only need to fix the permissions for your user session, so not a big deal (systemd might overwrite them though) and you may need to repeat the operation
- you should be able to override the umask for the systemd user session (https://github.com/systemd/systemd/issues/16963#issuecomment-689656886))


I am closing the issue since there is not much we can do here, but I'll open an issue for crun to improve the error message: https://github.com/containers/crun/issues/1736

@giuseppe Thanks for everything!

Just one more quick question: In case one does not need to set their own resource limits, is there anything lost (functionality-wise or security-wise) when cgroups are disabled for podman (`--cgroups=disabled`)?

- Using cgroupfs results in the same error msg (and is probably not the best idea on a systemd system?).
- For security reasons, I really don't want to have any "real" user id's mapped into the container as it increases the attack surface.
- I will have to closer look at which directories may need fixing in the user's cgroup user slice. A quick(ish) look and test was disappointing thus far.
- Setting the user session's umask to 0022 kind of defeats the purpose of setting a stricter umask system-wide in the first place. ;-)

> Just one more quick question: In case one does not need to set their own resource limits, is there anything lost (functionality-wise or security-wise) when cgroups are disabled for podman (`--cgroups=disabled`)?

not really, unless you need to use `--pid=host`, in that case a cgroup is necessary to track the processes in the container
